learn_module.description: Learn to identify households eligible for bed net distribution based on the presence of children under 5 and pregnant women.

questions.label: Eligible households have children under 5 or pregnant women. Verify eligibility through observation and interviews. Document all eligible members and current bed net status before registering.
A household has 3 children ages 2, 7, and 12. Is this household eligible for bed nets?
questions.hint: Eligibility targets specific vulnerable groups: children under 5 and pregnant women.

options.label: Only if they have no nets

questions.label: A woman is 4 months pregnant with no children under 5. Is her household eligible?
questions.hint: Consider all categories of eligible household members, not just children.

options.label: No, only households with children qualify

questions.label: You visit a household and nobody is home. What should you do?
questions.hint: Every visit attempt must be documented for program tracking.

options.label: Schedule a return visit and note the attempt
Skip and move to the next household

questions.label: The household head reports 2 children under 5, but you only see 1. What should you do?
questions.hint: Accurate registration requires verifying all household members.

options.label: Ask to verify all children present or absent
Record only the child you see

questions.label: A household has 1 pregnant woman and 3 children under 5. How many eligible members total?
questions.hint: Each eligible individual is counted separately for net allocation.

questions.label: A household has 2 existing nets but 4 eligible members. What do you record?
questions.hint: Complete documentation of both existing nets and eligible members supports accurate need assessment.

questions.label: The household head refuses to share family information. What is your best response?
questions.hint: Building community trust is essential. Participation must always be voluntary.

options.label: Explain program benefits and respect their decision
Report them to the village leader
Mark as ineligible and move on

questions.label: An elderly couple has no children under 5 or pregnant women. Are they eligible?
questions.hint: Eligibility is strictly defined by program criteria for vulnerable groups.

options.label: No, they don't meet eligibility criteria
Only if they previously had nets

questions.calculate: round((if(/data/q1 = 'yes_under5', 1, 0) + if(/data/q2 = 'yes_pregnant', 1, 0) + if(/data/q3 = 'return_visit', 1, 0) + if(/data/q4 = 'verify_children', 1, 0) + if(/data/q5 = 'four_members', 1, 0) + if(/data/q6 = 'record_both', 1, 0) + if(/data/q7 = 'explain_benefits', 1, 0) + if(/data/q8 = 'not_eligible', 1, 0)) div 8 * 100)

learn_module.description: Learn the correct procedures for distributing insecticide-treated bed nets, including allocation rules, documentation, and recipient acknowledgment.

questions.label: Each eligible household receives one ITN per two eligible members. Document recipient identity, quantities, and old net condition. Always obtain acknowledgment before completing distribution.
A household has 4 eligible members. How many nets should they receive?
questions.hint: The allocation formula is one net per two eligible members.

questions.label: The household head is away but the spouse is present. Can you distribute nets?
questions.hint: Any responsible adult household member can receive nets on behalf of the family.

options.label: Yes, give to the spouse and record their name
No, only the household head can receive
Leave nets and return for documentation

questions.label: A household's existing nets have large holes. How do you record their condition?
questions.hint: Net condition affects the total need calculation for the household.

questions.label: The recipient cannot sign the acknowledgment form. What do you do?
questions.hint: Alternative forms of acknowledgment are acceptable in the program.

options.label: Record a thumbprint or witness acknowledgment

questions.label: You recorded the wrong number of nets distributed. What should you do?
questions.hint: Data accuracy is critical for program inventory and tracking.

options.label: Correct the record immediately and note the change
Leave it - small errors don't matter
Ask your supervisor to fix it later

questions.label: A household asks for extra nets for visiting relatives. What do you do?
questions.hint: Net allocation follows strict program rules based on registered eligible members.

options.label: Explain nets are for registered eligible members only
Give extra nets to be helpful
Register the visitors as household members
Promise to return with more nets

questions.label: You discover a household was incorrectly registered as eligible. What do you do?
questions.hint: Only verified eligible households should receive nets to maintain program integrity.

options.label: Do not distribute and update the record

questions.label: What must be recorded for every net distribution?
questions.hint: Complete documentation protects both the CHW and the program.

options.label: Recipient name, quantity, date, and acknowledgment

questions.calculate: round((if(/data/q1 = 'two_nets', 1, 0) + if(/data/q2 = 'yes_record_spouse', 1, 0) + if(/data/q3 = 'damaged_replace', 1, 0) + if(/data/q4 = 'thumbprint', 1, 0) + if(/data/q5 = 'correct_now', 1, 0) + if(/data/q6 = 'eligible_only', 1, 0) + if(/data/q7 = 'do_not_distribute', 1, 0) + if(/data/q8 = 'all_details', 1, 0)) div 8 * 100)

learn_module.description: Learn the correct procedures for follow-up verification visits, including timing, compliance assessment, and counseling households on proper bed net use.

questions.label: Follow-up visits happen 2 weeks after distribution. Verify nets are hung over sleeping areas, check for damage, and confirm eligible members sleep under nets. Counsel on proper use if needed.
When should you conduct the first follow-up visit after distribution?
questions.hint: The program specifies a standard follow-up timeline for all households.

options.label: Only if the household requests it

questions.label: You find the distributed net still in its package, unused. What do you do?
questions.hint: Non-use is an opportunity for education and hands-on support, not punishment.

options.label: Counsel on importance and demonstrate installation

questions.label: A net is hung but doesn't properly cover the sleeping area. What do you record?
questions.hint: Partial compliance should be documented accurately with corrective action taken.

questions.label: The household reports the net tore after one week. What should you do?
questions.hint: Documenting damage details and cause helps the program improve net quality.

options.label: Record damage, assess cause, report for replacement
Tell them to repair it themselves
Immediately give a replacement from your stock

questions.label: Two of 3 eligible children are sleeping under nets. How do you record usage?
questions.hint: Record actual numbers for accurate compliance tracking across the program.

questions.label: A household is using the bed net for fishing instead of sleeping. What is the best action?
questions.hint: Net misuse is a common challenge. Education is the primary intervention strategy.

options.label: Counsel on proper use and health risks of misuse
Ignore it and mark as compliant

questions.label: Only a teenager is home during your verification visit. What do you do?
questions.hint: Some observations can be made directly, but full verification needs a responsible adult.

options.label: Observe net installation, schedule return for full check
Ask the teenager to answer all questions

questions.label: All nets are properly installed and all members sleep under them. What is the case outcome?
questions.hint: Successful verification with full compliance leads to case completion.

questions.calculate: round((if(/data/q1 = 'two_weeks', 1, 0) + if(/data/q2 = 'counsel_demo', 1, 0) + if(/data/q3 = 'improper_guide', 1, 0) + if(/data/q4 = 'record_report', 1, 0) + if(/data/q5 = 'partial', 1, 0) + if(/data/q6 = 'counsel_misuse', 1, 0) + if(/data/q7 = 'partial_return', 1, 0) + if(/data/q8 = 'verified_complete', 1, 0)) div 8 * 100)

idd_coverage.reasoning: All 3 specified modules present (Household Identification, Distribution Protocol, Follow-up Verification) with matching form names. All key field categories covered: scenario questions and scoring for Household ID, step ordering and documentation for Distribution Protocol, timing rules and compliance indicators for Verification. connect_type correctly set to 'learn', case_types correctly null. Minor gap: PDD specifies '15 minutes per lesson' implying substantial instructional content, but each lesson is a single summary paragraph label — adequate for a blueprint but thin for actual training delivery.

form_completeness.reasoning: Each form has 8 scenario-based select1 questions plus a lesson summary label and hidden score calculation — well-structured and sufficient for meaningful knowledge assessment. All questions marked required. Labels are clear, professional, and scenario-based. Question types are appropriate (select1 for multiple-choice quizzes). However, the instructional content before each quiz is a single label paragraph, which is minimal for a training module that should teach before assessing. A real learn app would benefit from multiple lesson screens with richer content preceding the quiz.

connect_config.reasoning: All Connect-specific configurations are correct and internally consistent. connect_type is 'learn'. Every form has learn_module with a meaningful description and assessment block with score_question referencing the actual hidden 'score' question id. passing_score of 80 aligns with the PDD's >80% requirement. Form type is 'survey' throughout, which is correct for learn modules. No misconfigurations detected.

data_model.reasoning: Learn app correctly has case_types set to null. No case management is needed for a training/assessment app, and none is incorrectly included. This is the correct data model for this app type.

validation_logic.reasoning: Score calculations are syntactically correct XPath: each uses if() for answer checking, sums correct answers, divides by 8, multiplies by 100, and wraps in round(). All correct answer values match the intended correct options. required='true()' is set on all quiz questions. Two issues: (1) Score granularity is coarse — only 9 possible values (0, 13, 25, 38, 50, 63, 75, 88, 100), meaning the effective passing threshold is 88% (7/8 correct), not 80% as configured, since 6/8=75% fails. This is stricter than the PDD's >80% target. (2) No display logic exists to show correct/incorrect feedback after each question, which would enhance the learning experience but is not strictly required by the PDD.

field_usability.reasoning: Excellent field usability. Questions are realistic scenario-based situations CHWs would actually encounter (household head away, nets used for fishing, teenager home alone). Labels are jargon-free and clear. Each question has 4 well-differentiated options with plausible distractors that test understanding rather than memorization. Hints are provided on every question and guide thinking without giving away answers. Module ordering follows the logical field workflow (identify → distribute → verify). Form flow is intuitive. Minor gap: the single-paragraph lesson summary before each quiz may not adequately prepare a CHW who is truly learning the material for the first time.

score.summary: Strong blueprint that faithfully covers all PDD Learn app requirements with well-crafted scenario-based assessments. The three modules map exactly to the PDD specification with correct Connect configuration throughout. Main weaknesses are thin instructional content (single label per lesson) and coarse score granularity that makes the effective passing threshold 88% rather than the intended 80%. Overall a solid, field-ready quiz structure that would meaningfully assess CHW readiness.

questions.label: Register a new household. Collect the head of household's details, count children under 5 and pregnant women, and check existing bed nets to calculate how many new nets are needed.
Household Demographics

children.label: Head of household name
children.hint: Full name of the person who heads this household

children.label: Household GPS location
children.hint: Stand near the household entrance and capture GPS

questions.label: Bed Net Needs Assessment

children.label: Children under 5
children.hint: Count all children under 5 years in this household

children.label: Pregnant women
children.hint: Count all currently pregnant women in this household

children.label: Existing nets in good condition
children.hint: Count bed nets currently owned that are not torn or damaged

questions.label: This household needs ${/data/needs_assessment/num_nets_needed} bed net(s).
This household already has enough bed nets. No distribution needed.
Distribute bed nets to this household. Confirm the number needed, record how many you give out, and get acknowledgment from the recipient.
Household Summary
Distribution Details

children.label: Nets distributed
children.hint: Enter the number of nets given to this household

children.label: Recipient name
children.hint: Full name of the person who received the nets

children.label: Condition of old nets
children.hint: Assess the condition of any existing nets in the household

children.label: Recipient acknowledged receipt?
children.hint: Confirm the recipient verbally acknowledged receiving the nets

questions.label: Conduct a follow-up visit (2 weeks after distribution). Visually inspect that bed nets are installed over sleeping areas and ask if they are being used nightly.
Household Summary
Verification Details

children.label: Are the nets installed?
children.hint: Visually check if nets are hung over sleeping areas

children.label: Why are some/all nets not installed?
children.hint: Ask the household why nets have not been hung up

children.label: Are the nets being used nightly?
children.hint: Ask if household members slept under the nets last night

children.label: Why are some/all nets not being used?
children.hint: Common reasons: too hot, no mosquitoes, stored for later

properties.name: num_nets_needed
properties.label: Nets Needed
properties.hint: Calculated: children under 5 + pregnant women - existing good nets (min 0)

idd_coverage.reasoning: All three PDD-specified services are present as distinct modules: household registration (demographics, GPS, bed net count), bed net distribution (quantities, recipients, old net condition), and follow-up verification (installation, usage, condition). All seven PDD case properties are captured. The three-step workflow (register→distribute→verify) is faithfully implemented with case list filters enforcing the correct sequencing. The 'needs_revisit' status is a sensible addition beyond the PDD's four-state lifecycle. Minor gap: no explicit scheduling mechanism between registration and distribution, though the case list filter functionally serves this purpose.

form_completeness.reasoning: All three forms have comprehensive question sets with correct types (text, int, phone, geopoint, select1, hidden, label). Required fields are correctly marked — all demographic and assessment fields are required, optional fields like phone_number are not. Labels are clear and professional. Good use of groups to organize related questions (demographics, needs assessment, distribution details, verification details). Follow-up forms include summary display labels showing previously collected case data, giving CHWs context. Intro labels on each form clearly explain the task. Minor: the distribution form could benefit from a photo capture of old nets or distribution receipt, but this wasn't specified in the PDD.

connect_config.reasoning: connect_type correctly set to 'deliver'. All three forms have deliver_unit with descriptive labels. Distribution and verification forms correctly have connect_task: true for payment tracking. Close case logic is properly configured with question/answer reference. Notable gap: the registration form lacks connect_task: true — per the PDD, CHWs are paid $2 per household visit including registration, so registration visits should also be trackable as connect tasks for payment. This could cause registration visits to go uncompensated.

data_model.reasoning: Case type 'household' with case_name_property 'head_of_household' is correct. All seven PDD-specified properties are present (head_of_household, num_children_under_5, num_pregnant_women, num_nets_needed, num_nets_distributed, nets_installed, nets_in_use) plus valuable additions (village, gps_location, phone_number, existing_good_nets, recipient_name, old_nets_condition, dates, reasons). The case_types schema at the bottom is thorough with data types, constraints, and option sets. Case properties are consistently mapped from form questions via case_property. The lifecycle (registered→nets_distributed→needs_revisit/verified→closed) correctly extends the PDD's four states with a practical 'needs_revisit' state. is_case_name: true correctly placed on head_of_household.

validation_logic.reasoning: Strong validation throughout. Numeric constraints are sensible: children 0-20, pregnant women 0-10, existing nets 0-50, distributed nets 1-20. The num_nets_needed calculation correctly uses max(0, children + pregnant - existing). Case list filters correctly enforce workflow: distribution only shows registered households with nets needed, verification shows distributed or needs-revisit households. Conditional display logic works correctly: nets_needed_display vs no_nets_needed_display, non_install_reason shown only when nets not all installed, non_use_reason shown only when nets not all in use. Close case logic correctly closes only when all nets installed AND all in use. Status transitions are correct across all three forms. XPath expressions are syntactically valid. Minor: the verification form requires nets_in_use even when nets_installed='none', where skip logic could avoid a redundant question.

field_usability.reasoning: Labels are clear and jargon-free — a CHW with basic training could use this. Hints provide practical guidance ('Stand near the household entrance and capture GPS', 'Common reasons: too hot, no mosquitoes, stored for later'). Form flow is logical within each module and across the workflow. Select options are comprehensive and unambiguous. Case list columns are well-chosen for each module's context (e.g., verification shows distribution date to help CHWs identify 2-week-old distributions). The display of previous case data in follow-up forms prevents CHWs from needing to remember details. Two gaps: (1) no mechanism to enforce the PDD's 2-week timing between distribution and verification — CHWs could verify too early; (2) the verification form asks about net usage even when no nets are installed, which would confuse field workers.

score.summary: This is a high-quality blueprint that faithfully translates the PDD requirements into a well-structured CommCare application. The three-module workflow with case list filters enforcing the correct lifecycle is well-designed, and the data model comprehensively captures all specified and derived fields. The main gaps are the missing connect_task on the registration form (which could affect CHW payment), and minor field-level usability issues around skip logic in the verification form.
